title: Psychoeducation: The CBT Model Overview
content: The CBT model proposes that psychological distress arises not from situations themselves but from how people interpret those situations. The cognitive triad (Beck, 1979) describes negative automatic thoughts about the self ('I am a failure'), the world ('Nothing goes right'), and the future ('Things will never improve') as central to depression. The model further identifies intermediate beliefs (rules, assumptions, attitudes) and underlying core beliefs that shape automatic thinking. CBT targets cognitions, behaviours, and physiological responses in an integrated way. Psychoeducation about this model helps clients become active agents in understanding and changing their own distress.
short_summary: Introduces the CBT cognitive triad model and how psychoeducation empowers clients to understand the thought-emotion-behaviour link.

title: Socratic Questioning in CBT
content: Socratic questioning is the primary conversational technique through which CBT therapists guide clients to examine their thinking. Rather than directly challenging or correcting thoughts, the therapist asks guided discovery questions: 'What is the evidence for that?', 'What would you tell a friend in the same situation?', 'What is the worst / most realistic / best that could happen?', 'How does thinking this way affect you?'. The goal is to promote metacognitive reflection and help clients arrive at more balanced perspectives independently, which is more durable than therapist-provided reframes. Effective Socratic dialogue is curious, collaborative, and non-confrontational.
short_summary: Describes Socratic questioning as a guided discovery technique for collaborative cognitive restructuring in CBT.

title: CBT Case Formulation
content: A CBT case formulation (conceptualisation) organises information about a client into a coherent model that guides treatment. The 5 Ps framework covers: Presenting problems, Predisposing factors (early experiences, genetics), Precipitating factors (what triggered current episode), Perpetuating factors (what maintains the problem), and Protective factors (strengths, resources). The formulation identifies core beliefs, intermediate beliefs, compensatory strategies, and the typical cognitive-behavioural-physiological cycle maintaining distress. A good formulation is collaborative (shared with the client), parsimonious, and generates clear treatment targets. It is revised as new information emerges.
short_summary: Explains the CBT 5 Ps case formulation framework linking early experience to current maintaining cycles and treatment targets.

title: Graded Task Assignment for Depression
content: Graded task assignment (GTA) is a CBT technique for depression in which a complex or overwhelming task is broken into small, sequential steps that progressively increase in difficulty. GTA directly addresses the depressive conviction 'I cannot do anything' by providing repeated mastery experiences. The therapist collaborates with the client to identify a meaningful target task, break it into micro-steps, predict likely obstacles, and debrief outcomes. Mastery ratings (0–10) and pleasure ratings are recorded after each step. GTA is distinguished from standard activity scheduling by its explicit grading and the therapeutic focus on disconfirming helplessness beliefs.
short_summary: Describes graded task assignment as a step-by-step behavioural intervention targeting depressive helplessness through mastery experiences.
contraindications: Severe psychomotor retardation or acute suicidality requires higher-level care first.

title: Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction: Body Scan
content: The body scan is a foundational MBSR practice developed by Jon Kabat-Zinn. The practitioner lies down and systematically directs non-judgmental awareness to different body regions, from the toes to the crown of the head, observing sensations without trying to change them. The body scan cultivates interoceptive awareness, reduces automatic tension patterns, and strengthens the capacity to observe uncomfortable sensations without reacting. Regular practice (45 minutes daily in the standard MBSR programme) reduces perceived stress, cortisol reactivity, and somatic symptoms in clinical and non-clinical populations. A brief 10-minute variant can be taught as a portable stress management tool.
short_summary: Introduces the MBSR body scan practice for developing non-judgmental interoceptive awareness and reducing stress reactivity.
contraindications: Trauma history — body-focused practices may trigger dissociation; use trauma-sensitive adaptations.

title: Progressive Muscle Relaxation
content: Progressive muscle relaxation (PMR), developed by Jacobson and refined by Wolpe, involves systematically tensing and releasing 16 (or 8 or 4) muscle groups to achieve deep muscular relaxation. The contrast between tension and relaxation heightens awareness of physiological arousal and provides voluntary control over it. A full PMR session takes 20–30 minutes; with practice clients learn abbreviated versions for daily use. PMR is a component of applied relaxation (Öst), systematic desensitisation, and sleep hygiene protocols. It is effective for generalised anxiety, specific phobia, chronic pain, insomnia, and tension headaches.
short_summary: Describes the PMR technique of systematic tension-release across muscle groups for anxiety and stress reduction.
contraindications: Musculoskeletal injuries — skip affected muscle groups.

title: Schema Therapy: Early Maladaptive Schemas
content: Young's Schema Therapy identifies 18 early maladaptive schemas (EMS) organised in five domains: Disconnection and Rejection (e.g., Abandonment, Mistrust/Abuse, Emotional Deprivation, Defectiveness/Shame, Social Isolation); Impaired Autonomy and Performance (Dependence, Vulnerability, Enmeshment, Failure); Impaired Limits (Entitlement, Insufficient Self-Control); Other-Directedness (Subjugation, Self-Sacrifice, Approval-Seeking); Overvigilance and Inhibition (Negativity/Pessimism, Emotional Inhibition, Unrelenting Standards, Punitiveness). EMS are identified via the Young Schema Questionnaire and addressed using cognitive, experiential, and behavioural interventions, including limited reparenting and imagery rescripting.
short_summary: Introduces Young's 18 early maladaptive schemas across five domains and the multi-modal schema therapy intervention framework.
contraindications: Schema therapy for personality disorder requires extended training; not for brief CBT episodes.

title: Imagery Rescripting for Trauma Memories
content: Imagery rescripting (ImRs) involves guiding the client to revisit a distressing memory image and then actively modify it to create a more adaptive outcome. The technique addresses the meaning encoded in traumatic memories rather than habituation alone. Stages include: (1) vividly reimagining the original scene; (2) identifying the key stuck-point meaning; (3) introducing a 'rescuer' (often the current adult self) into the image to protect or comfort the younger self; (4) creating a new image where needs are met. ImRs reduces intrusive imagery, shame, and negative belief conviction in PTSD, social anxiety, depression, and eating disorders.
short_summary: Describes imagery rescripting as a trauma memory modification technique that targets meaning and shame via adaptive new imagery.
safety_notes: Requires clinical training; can increase distress transiently.
contraindications: Active psychosis; severe dissociation without specialist support.

title: Interoceptive Exposure for Panic Disorder
content: Interoceptive exposure (IE) targets the feared bodily sensations in panic disorder by deliberately inducing them in a controlled setting. Common exercises include spinning in a chair (dizziness), breathing through a narrow straw (breathlessness), running on the spot (elevated heart rate), and hyperventilation (tingling, lightheadedness). The rationale is that fear of sensations — not the sensations themselves — drives panic. IE combined with psychoeducation and cognitive restructuring produces rapid reduction in panic frequency and agoraphobic avoidance. A hierarchy of interoceptive exercises is constructed based on SUDS ratings, proceeding from tolerable to most feared sensations.
short_summary: Explains interoceptive exposure for panic disorder: deliberately inducing feared sensations to disconfirm catastrophic misinterpretations.
safety_notes: Medical clearance required before hyperventilation exercises in clients with cardiac or respiratory conditions.
contraindications: Cardiovascular conditions; asthma; pregnancy.

title: CBT for Insomnia: Sleep Restriction Therapy
content: Sleep restriction therapy (SRT) is the most potent component of CBT-I. It involves initially limiting time in bed (TIB) to the client's actual average sleep time (minimum 5 hours) to consolidate fragmented sleep and build homeostatic sleep drive. Sleep efficiency (SE = time asleep / TIB × 100) is calculated from sleep diaries. When SE exceeds 85% for five consecutive nights, TIB is extended by 15–20 minutes. The process continues until optimal sleep duration is achieved. SRT is counterintuitive and temporarily increases sleepiness, so client education and motivation enhancement are essential. It produces durable insomnia remission superior to sleep medication.
short_summary: Describes sleep restriction therapy (SRT) from CBT-I: limiting time in bed to build sleep drive, then titrating upward as efficiency improves.
safety_notes: Increases daytime sleepiness initially — caution with drivers and heavy machinery operators.
contraindications: Bipolar disorder; seizure disorders; parasomnias — requires modification.

title: Cognitive Defusion Techniques in ACT
content: Cognitive defusion in ACT refers to techniques that alter the function of thoughts rather than their content, reducing the literal believability and behavioural impact of thoughts without requiring the client to accept them as true or dispute them. Defusion techniques include: the 'leaves on a stream' visualisation (placing thoughts on leaves and watching them float by); singing the thought to a tune; adding 'I am having the thought that…' prefix; thanking the mind ('Thank you, mind, for that thought'); and the 'milk, milk, milk' exercise (rapidly repeating a word until it loses meaning). Defusion reduces emotional reactivity to difficult thoughts and increases values-based action.
short_summary: Explains ACT cognitive defusion techniques that reduce thought believability and reactivity without challenging thought content.

title: Values Clarification in ACT
content: Values in ACT are chosen life directions — not goals (which can be achieved) but ongoing qualities of action (e.g., 'being a loving parent', 'living with integrity'). Values clarification exercises help clients identify what truly matters to them, often obscured by avoidance and social compliance. Common techniques include: the 'epitaph exercise' (what do you want to be remembered for?); the 'dear friend' letter; the values card sort; and the 'life domains' matrix (work, relationships, health, personal growth). Once values are clarified, committed action — small, concrete steps in valued directions even in the presence of discomfort — becomes the therapeutic focus.
short_summary: Describes ACT values clarification exercises and how identified values drive committed action in the presence of difficult thoughts and feelings.

title: DBT Mindfulness: What and How Skills
content: DBT core mindfulness skills are divided into 'What' skills (what you do) and 'How' skills (how you do it). What skills: Observe (noticing experience without words), Describe (labelling what is noticed without judgment), Participate (engaging fully in an activity). How skills: Non-judgmentally (dropping evaluative labels — noting facts without 'good' or 'bad'), One-mindfully (doing one thing at a time with full attention), Effectively (doing what works in the current situation rather than what is 'right'). These skills build a mindful foundation for all other DBT modules. Short daily practices (2–3 minutes) are assigned as homework, with diary cards to track practice.
short_summary: Describes DBT core mindfulness What skills (Observe, Describe, Participate) and How skills (Non-judgmentally, One-mindfully, Effectively).

title: DBT Emotion Regulation: PLEASE Skills
content: PLEASE skills are DBT emotion regulation skills targeting physical vulnerability to emotional dysregulation: treat PhysicaL illness; balance Eating; avoid mood-Altering substances; balance Sleep; get Exercise. Each skill is supported by psychoeducation: illness disrupts emotion regulation; blood sugar instability worsens mood; alcohol/drugs lower distress tolerance; sleep deprivation amplifies emotional reactivity; exercise reduces baseline arousal and increases positive affect. Clients complete PLEASE skill tracking cards. PLEASE skills are foundational — they reduce vulnerability to emotional crises, making other DBT skills more accessible.
short_summary: Describes DBT PLEASE skills targeting physical health foundations (illness, eating, substances, sleep, exercise) that reduce emotional vulnerability.

title: Compassion-Focused Therapy: Soothing Rhythm Breathing
content: Soothing rhythm breathing (SRB) is a foundational CFT technique for activating the parasympathetic soothing/affiliative system. The client breathes slightly deeper and slower than normal (approximately 5–6 breath cycles per minute), with a gentle pause between exhale and inhale. The focus is on cultivating a quality of warmth and settling rather than achievement. SRB directly reduces physiological arousal from the threat system (sympathetic) and is taught at the beginning of each CFT session as a 'compassion doorway'. It primes the nervous system for imagery, self-compassion, and emotional exploration work. Regular daily practice is recommended.
short_summary: Describes CFT soothing rhythm breathing (approx. 5–6 cycles/min) as a parasympathetic activation technique preceding compassion work.
contraindications: Respiratory conditions — adapt pacing; severe dissociation — monitor carefully.

title: Narrative Exposure Therapy: Lifeline Technique
content: Narrative Exposure Therapy (NET) was developed for complex trauma and refugee populations. The lifeline technique is its opening phase: the client constructs a physical lifeline on the floor or paper using stones (representing negative/traumatic events) and flowers (representing positive events) placed along a rope or line representing their life chronologically. This externalises the life story, provides a safe overview before detailed processing, and orients the therapist to major trauma and resilience periods. The lifeline is collaboratively mapped and discussed. Subsequent NET sessions involve narrating and documenting each major event in chronological order, integrating fragmented trauma memories into a coherent autobiographical narrative.
short_summary: Introduces NET's lifeline technique as a chronological mapping of traumatic and positive life events that orients trauma narrative work.
safety_notes: Trained NET therapist required; not suitable for self-guided work.
contraindications: Active psychosis; acute suicidality requiring stabilisation.

title: Interpersonal Therapy: Grief Role
content: Interpersonal Therapy (IPT) addresses four problem areas linked to depression onset: grief, role transitions, role disputes, and interpersonal deficits. The grief focus area targets complicated mourning following bereavement. IPT for grief involves: facilitating the normal mourning process by encouraging discussion of the deceased and the loss; exploring ambivalent feelings; gradually helping the client re-engage with social activities and rebuild relationships. Unlike prolonged grief treatment, IPT does not involve direct exposure to avoided grief-related stimuli but instead focuses on interpersonal consequences of the loss and re-establishing connections.
short_summary: Describes IPT grief focus area: facilitating mourning, exploring ambivalence, and re-engaging socially after bereavement-linked depression.

title: Interpersonal Therapy: Role Transitions
content: In IPT, role transitions encompass major life changes that disrupt social roles and self-concept, triggering depression. Examples include: job loss or promotion, relationship transitions (marriage, divorce, parenthood), moving city, retirement, illness. IPT for role transitions involves: mourning the loss of the old role; exploring ambivalent feelings about both the old and new role; identifying skills and supports needed in the new role; and building a social network appropriate to the new role. The therapist helps the client reframe the transition as an opportunity for growth rather than purely a loss, while validating the real challenges involved.
short_summary: Describes IPT role transitions focus area addressing depression triggered by major life changes through mourning old roles and building new ones.

title: Psychoeducation on the Stress Response
content: The fight-or-flight-freeze response is an evolutionarily conserved survival mechanism mediated by the sympathetic nervous system and HPA axis. Stressors activate the amygdala, triggering adrenaline and cortisol release, increasing heart rate and respiration, diverting blood to large muscle groups, and sharpening threat attention. These changes are adaptive for acute physical threat but maladaptive when chronically activated by psychological stressors. Chronic stress contributes to anxiety disorders, depression, cardiovascular disease, immune suppression, and cognitive impairment. CBT psychoeducation normalises stress responses and introduces the rationale for relaxation, mindfulness, and cognitive interventions.
short_summary: Explains the fight-or-flight-freeze stress response, its adaptive and maladaptive aspects, and the rationale for CBT stress interventions.

title: Worry and Generalised Anxiety: Intolerance of Uncertainty
content: Intolerance of uncertainty (IU) is a core maintaining factor in GAD. Clients with high IU treat uncertainty as threatening and unacceptable, leading to chronic worry as an attempt to resolve or prepare for every possible outcome. The CBT/IU model (Dugas) targets IU directly through: psychoeducation on the function of worry; the 'what if' survey (testing whether uncertainty actually produces harm); uncertainty exposure (gradually approaching uncertain situations without resolving them prematurely); and cognitive restructuring of beliefs about the necessity of certainty. IU interventions are distinct from, and complementary to, worry postponement.
short_summary: Describes the intolerance of uncertainty (IU) model of GAD and cognitive-behavioural interventions targeting IU directly.

title: Body Image Interventions in CBT
content: Negative body image involves overvaluation of appearance, selective attention to disliked features, avoidance of body-related situations, and frequent body-checking behaviours. CBT body image interventions include: mirror exposure with descriptive (not evaluative) commentary to reduce avoidance and shift from critical to neutral attention; body-checking response prevention; activity exposure (wearing revealing clothing, swimming); and cognitive restructuring of appearance-based self-worth beliefs. The 'appearance assumptions questionnaire' identifies core schemas. Body image disturbance is central to eating disorders, social anxiety, and depression; addressing it improves global self-esteem and quality of life.
short_summary: Describes CBT body image interventions including mirror exposure, checking response prevention, and restructuring appearance-based self-worth beliefs.
contraindications: Active eating disorder — body image work should be coordinated with comprehensive ED treatment.

title: Mindfulness-Based Cognitive Therapy: Relapse Prevention
content: Mindfulness-Based Cognitive Therapy (MBCT) was developed by Segal, Williams, and Teasdale specifically for relapse prevention in recurrent depression. It is delivered in an 8-week group programme combining mindfulness meditation practices (sitting meditation, body scan, mindful movement) with CBT psychoeducation and cognitive techniques. MBCT targets the ruminative thinking style and depressive mood-thought reactivation patterns that drive relapse. The key mechanism is decentring — the capacity to observe depressive thoughts as passing mental events rather than facts. MBCT is recommended by NICE for clients with three or more depressive episodes and reduces relapse rates by approximately 50%.
short_summary: Describes MBCT as an 8-week group programme combining mindfulness and CBT for recurrent depression relapse prevention via decentring.
contraindications: Active severe depression — individual therapy or pharmacotherapy may be needed first.

title: Agoraphobia: Graduated In-Vivo Exposure
content: Agoraphobia is the fear and avoidance of situations perceived as difficult to escape or where help might not be available during a panic attack (crowded places, public transport, open spaces, queues). Graduated in-vivo exposure is the primary CBT intervention. A hierarchy of avoided situations is constructed (SUDS 0–100), and the client progresses from least to most feared, initially with therapist support and then independently. Each exposure is held until anxiety reduces by at least 50%. Safety behaviours (phone in hand, specific companion required, escape route planning) are systematically eliminated. Partner-assisted exposure is incorporated when avoidance involves accommodation by significant others.
short_summary: Describes graduated in-vivo exposure for agoraphobia with safety behaviour elimination and partner-assisted protocols.
contraindications: Severe avoidance may require therapist-accompanied exposure before independent practice.

title: Emotion-Focused Couples Therapy: Cycle Interruption
content: Emotionally Focused Couples Therapy (EFT, Johnson) identifies negative interactional cycles — typically pursue-withdraw or criticise-defend patterns — that reflect unmet attachment needs. Cycle interruption involves: (1) tracking the negative cycle in session as it unfolds; (2) reflecting each partner's position in the cycle and the emotion underneath their behaviour; (3) accessing the primary (softer) emotion beneath secondary (expressed) emotions; (4) re-framing the cycle as the problem, not the partner. Slowing the cycle creates space for each partner to hear the other's vulnerability. EFT is recommended for relationship distress and is transdiagnostic for depression and anxiety in relational contexts.
short_summary: Describes EFT negative cycle interruption: tracking pursue-withdraw patterns and accessing primary attachment emotions beneath reactive responses.
safety_notes: Screen for domestic violence; couples therapy contraindicated in active abuse situations.
contraindications: Active domestic violence; one partner with active untreated psychosis.

title: Parent-Child CBT: Managing Childhood Anxiety
content: CBT for childhood anxiety (e.g., the FRIENDS programme, Coping Cat) combines psychoeducation, cognitive restructuring, graded exposure, and parent training. The CBT model for children is taught using age-appropriate language and activities. Parents are included because parental accommodation (reassurance-giving, avoiding anxiety-provoking situations) maintains child anxiety. Parent training focuses on: modelling approach behaviour; withdrawing reassurance gradually; reinforcing brave behaviour; and managing own anxiety to avoid transmission. Exposure hierarchies are built collaboratively with child and parent and progressed via reward charts. CBT for childhood anxiety has strong RCT evidence.
short_summary: Outlines CBT for childhood anxiety including child-adapted formulation, parent training to reduce accommodation, and reward-supported exposure.
contraindications: Severe family dysfunction may require systemic intervention before individual CBT.

title: Metacognitive Therapy: CAS Interruption
content: Wells's Metacognitive Therapy (MCT) proposes that the Cognitive Attentional Syndrome (CAS) — comprising worry, rumination, threat monitoring, and unhelpful coping — is maintained by positive metacognitive beliefs ('Worrying helps me prepare') and negative metacognitive beliefs ('My worrying is uncontrollable'). MCT targets the CAS directly rather than the content of worries. Key techniques include: detached mindfulness (observing thoughts without engaging with them); attention training (ATT) to break threat monitoring; postponing worry; and challenging metacognitive beliefs via behavioural experiments (e.g., deliberately worrying more to test the 'uncontrollable' belief). MCT shows superior outcomes to CBT in some GAD trials.
short_summary: Describes MCT's Cognitive Attentional Syndrome (CAS) model and interventions including detached mindfulness, ATT, and metacognitive belief challenging.

title: Behavioural Activation: Activity Monitoring
content: Activity monitoring is the first step of behavioural activation for depression. The client records hourly activities over 7 days and rates each for mastery (sense of achievement, 0–10) and pleasure (enjoyment, 0–10). Analysis reveals: (a) the current pattern of activities; (b) activities correlated with better mood; (c) periods of inactivity or rumination; (d) balance between obligatory and voluntary activities. This baseline informs activity scheduling — increasing pleasure and mastery activities, reducing avoidance. Activity monitoring challenges the depressive belief 'Nothing gives me pleasure anymore' by finding genuine pleasure pockets and demonstrates the mood-activity relationship empirically.
short_summary: Describes weekly activity monitoring in BA for depression: logging activities with mastery/pleasure ratings to identify mood-activity patterns.

title: Specific Phobia: Single-Session Intensive Exposure
content: Intensive single-session treatment (SST) for specific phobia (Öst, 1989) delivers a complete graded exposure hierarchy in one 3-hour session, achieving outcomes equivalent to multi-session treatment. The protocol includes: psychoeducation on anxiety and avoidance; collaborative construction of a 10-step hierarchy; stepped in-vivo exposure with the therapist physically present and progressively withdrawing support; inhibitory learning principles (maximising expectancy violation, using variability); and post-session relapse prevention. SST is effective for animal phobias, blood-injection-injury phobia, claustrophobia, and height phobia. It is highly cost-effective and suitable for primary care.
short_summary: Describes Öst's single-session intensive exposure treatment for specific phobia using a 3-hour graded hierarchy with inhibitory learning principles.
contraindications: Blood-injection-injury phobia — use applied tension technique to prevent vasovagal syncope.

title: Applied Tension for Blood-Injection-Injury Phobia
content: Blood-injection-injury (BII) phobia has a unique two-phase physiological response: initial sympathetic activation followed by parasympathetic-driven vasovagal syncope (fainting). Standard exposure is insufficient because fainting is the primary feared outcome. Applied tension (Öst & Sterner, 1987) involves the client tensing large muscle groups (arms, torso, legs) for 15 seconds, then releasing, and repeating 5 times. This raises blood pressure, preventing the vasovagal drop. Applied tension is combined with in-vivo exposure (viewing blood images, then attending medical procedures) once the technique is mastered. It achieves remission rates of 80–90% in randomised trials.
short_summary: Explains applied tension technique for BII phobia: muscle tensing to prevent vasovagal fainting during blood/injury exposure.
safety_notes: Screen for cardiovascular conditions before applying muscle tension.
contraindications: Cardiovascular conditions — medical clearance required.

title: Psychoeducation on Cognitive Distortions
content: Burns (1980) popularised a taxonomy of cognitive distortions that clients learn to identify in their automatic thoughts: All-or-nothing thinking (seeing things in black-and-white); Overgeneralisation (drawing broad conclusions from a single event); Mental filter (focusing exclusively on negatives); Disqualifying the positive; Mind reading (assuming others think negatively); Fortune telling (predicting negative outcomes); Magnification/Minimisation (catastrophising or trivialising); Emotional reasoning ('I feel it, so it must be true'); Should statements (rigid rules about self and others); Labelling (global negative self-labels); Personalisation (taking excessive responsibility). Naming distortions helps clients create distance from automatic thoughts.
short_summary: Introduces Burns's 11 cognitive distortion categories as psychoeducational labels for identifying and distancing from automatic negative thoughts.
source_name: Burns Feeling Good; Beck Institute CBT Principles

title: Safety Planning for Suicide Risk
content: The Stanley-Brown Safety Planning Intervention (SPI) is a structured, collaborative tool for reducing suicidal crisis escalation. Components: (1) Warning signs — personal internal cues indicating escalating crisis; (2) Internal coping strategies the client can use alone; (3) Social contacts and settings that provide distraction; (4) People who can be asked for help; (5) Professionals and agencies to contact in crisis (including 24-hour lines); (6) Making the environment safe — means restriction. The plan is written in the client's own words, reviewed and practised, and kept accessible (phone). SPI reduces suicide attempts in high-risk populations and is suitable as a standalone or adjunct intervention.
short_summary: Describes the 6-component Stanley-Brown Safety Planning Intervention for suicidal crisis, including warning signs, coping strategies, and means restriction.
safety_notes: Not a substitute for comprehensive risk assessment. Refer to emergency services as indicated.
contraindications: Imminent intent with plan and means — requires emergency intervention.

title: Trauma: Window of Tolerance Model
content: The window of tolerance (WOT), introduced by Dan Siegel and developed in trauma therapy by Pat Ogden, describes the zone of arousal in which a person can effectively process experience. Within the window, the prefrontal cortex is online and emotions can be regulated. Hyperarousal (above WOT) — flashbacks, panic, rage — involves sympathetic activation overwhelming cortical function. Hypoarousal (below WOT) — dissociation, numbing, disconnection — involves dorsal vagal shutdown. Trauma stabilisation aims to widen the window through skills building (grounding, breathing, titrated trauma exposure) so that trauma processing can occur within the WOT. This model is central to phase-based trauma treatment.
short_summary: Introduces the window of tolerance model for trauma: optimal arousal zone for processing, with hyperarousal and hypoarousal outside windows.

title: Grounding Techniques for Dissociation
content: Grounding techniques anchor clients to the present moment during dissociative or overwhelmed states. Sensory grounding uses the 5-4-3-2-1 technique: name 5 things you see, 4 you can touch, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, 1 you taste. Physical grounding uses firm floor contact (pressing feet down, sitting in chair with back straight), cold water on the wrists, or holding an ice cube. Cognitive grounding uses orientation statements ('I am [name], it is [date], I am safe in [location]'). Movement grounding includes slow deliberate walking. Grounding is used preventively before trauma processing, during flooding, and as a self-regulation skill between sessions. Clients practice daily to build proficiency.
short_summary: Describes sensory, physical, cognitive, and movement grounding techniques for managing dissociation and overwhelm in trauma treatment.

title: Acceptance of Emotions: The Emotion Regulation Framework
content: Gross's process model of emotion regulation identifies five families of strategies: situation selection (choosing contexts to reduce emotion), situation modification (changing features of a situation), attentional deployment (directing attention away from or toward emotional stimuli), cognitive change (reappraisal — reinterpreting a situation's meaning), and response modulation (acting on physiological or behavioural emotion responses). Reappraisal is generally adaptive; suppression is generally maladaptive (social costs, physiological costs). CBT focuses primarily on reappraisal and acceptance. Acceptance-based strategies (allowing emotion without struggle) reduce secondary distress and are complementary to reappraisal for reducing primary emotional distress.
short_summary: Describes Gross's five emotion regulation strategy families and the evidence for reappraisal and acceptance over suppression.

title: EMDR: Phases Overview
content: Eye Movement Desensitisation and Reprocessing (EMDR) is an evidence-based trauma treatment recommended by NICE and WHO. The 8-phase protocol includes: (1) History-taking and case formulation; (2) Preparation (coping skills, window of tolerance stabilisation); (3) Assessment (target memory, negative cognition, positive cognition, emotions, body sensation, SUDS and VOC ratings); (4) Desensitisation (sets of bilateral stimulation while holding the target memory); (5) Installation (strengthening the positive cognition); (6) Body scan (scanning for residual body tension); (7) Closure (containment if processing incomplete); (8) Re-evaluation. Bilateral stimulation (eye movements, tapping, audio tones) is hypothesised to activate the orienting response and facilitate adaptive information processing.
short_summary: Outlines EMDR's 8-phase protocol for trauma: from history-taking through bilateral stimulation desensitisation to re-evaluation.
safety_notes: EMDR requires trained certified practitioner; premature activation without stabilisation can destabilise clients.
contraindications: Active psychosis; severe dissociative disorder — specialist adaptation required.

title: Cognitive Restructuring: Decatastrophising
content: Decatastrophising is a specific cognitive restructuring technique for catastrophic thinking. The therapist guides the client to: (1) identify the catastrophic prediction ('If I have a panic attack at work, everyone will think I'm crazy and I'll be fired'); (2) estimate the realistic probability of each step in the catastrophic chain; (3) examine the evidence for and against; (4) generate the realistic most likely outcome; (5) conduct a coping appraisal ('Even if X did happen, how would I cope?'). The coping appraisal is particularly important — it shifts focus from outcome probability to coping confidence. Pie charts can illustrate how small the probability of the catastrophic outcome actually is.
short_summary: Describes decatastrophising: probability estimation, evidence examination, realistic outcome generation, and coping appraisal for catastrophic thinking.

title: DBT FAST Skills for Self-Respect
content: FAST skills in DBT are interpersonal effectiveness skills for maintaining self-respect in relationships: be Fair (to oneself as well as to others); no Apologies (for having opinions, making requests, or existing — only apologise when genuinely wrong); Stick to values (do not compromise core values for relationship goals); be Truthful (do not lie, act helpless, or exaggerate). FAST skills address patterns of excessive self-sacrifice and approval-seeking that erode self-esteem and create resentment. They are used alongside DEAR MAN (for goal achievement) and GIVE (for relationship maintenance). FAST is particularly relevant for clients with self-sacrifice, subjugation, or approval-seeking schemas.
short_summary: Describes DBT FAST skills (Fair, no Apologies, Stick to values, Truthful) for maintaining self-respect in interpersonal interactions.

title: DBT GIVE Skills for Relationship Maintenance
content: GIVE skills in DBT prioritise maintaining the quality and warmth of a relationship over achieving a specific goal: be Gentle (no attacks, threats, or judgement); act Interested (listen actively, make eye contact, ask questions); Validate (acknowledge the other person's feelings and perspective as understandable given their situation); use an Easy manner (lighten the interaction with humour or warmth; do not be overly intense). GIVE skills are used when preserving the relationship is the primary goal and the immediate outcome is less important. They prevent conflict escalation and model the repair skills needed in close relationships. Often used alongside DEAR MAN and FAST.
short_summary: Describes DBT GIVE skills (Gentle, Interested, Validate, Easy manner) for maintaining relationship quality in interpersonal interactions.

title: Prolonged Grief Disorder: Revisiting Intervention
content: Prolonged grief disorder (PGD) is characterised by persistent, intense yearning for the deceased, difficulty accepting the death, bitterness, and functional impairment beyond 12 months post-loss. Shear's Complicated Grief Treatment (CGT) combines elements of IPT and exposure: aspirational goals work (rebuilding a life with meaning post-loss); revisiting exercises (imaginal revisiting of the death scene to reduce avoidance and process the loss); situational revisiting (graduated exposure to avoided grief reminders — places, people, activities associated with the deceased); and positive reminiscing. CGT is superior to standard IPT in reducing PGD severity.
short_summary: Describes Shear's CGT for prolonged grief disorder using aspirational goals, imaginal revisiting of the death, and situational exposure to grief reminders.
safety_notes: Screen for suicidality; grief and depression frequently co-occur.

title: Psychoeducation on Sleep Architecture
content: Providing psychoeducation on normal sleep architecture is a foundational step in CBT-I. Normal adult sleep consists of 4–5 cycles of approximately 90 minutes, each progressing through NREM stages (N1 light, N2 intermediate, N3 slow-wave deep) and REM sleep. Deep slow-wave sleep predominates in the first half of the night; REM predominates in the second half. Brief awakenings between cycles are normal. Sleep architecture changes with age — deep sleep reduces, awakenings increase. Many 'sleep problems' are reframed as normal variation once clients understand sleep architecture. Correcting misconceptions (e.g., 'I need 8 solid hours') reduces sleep-related anxiety and hyperarousal.
short_summary: Explains normal sleep architecture (NREM/REM cycles, stage distribution across the night) to correct sleep misconceptions in CBT-I.

title: Stimulus Control for Insomnia
content: Stimulus control (Bootzin, 1972) reconditioning the bed and bedroom as cues for sleep rather than for wakefulness, arousal, or frustration. Instructions: (1) go to bed only when sleepy; (2) use the bed only for sleep and sex; (3) if unable to sleep within 20 minutes, get up and go to another room — return only when sleepy; (4) repeat as needed; (5) maintain a consistent wake time regardless of sleep duration; (6) avoid daytime naps. Stimulus control breaks the learned association between the bed and wakefulness that perpetuates insomnia. It is one of the most empirically supported components of CBT-I.
short_summary: Describes Bootzin's stimulus control instructions for insomnia: reconditioning the bed as a sleep cue through consistent behavioural rules.
contraindications: Parasomnias — getting out of bed during sleep may be unsafe; modify instructions.

title: Interpersonal Effectiveness: Validation Strategies
content: Validation in DBT means communicating that a person's emotions, thoughts, or behaviours make sense given their history, biology, or the current situation — not that they are objectively correct. Linehan describes six levels of validation: Level 1 (paying attention), Level 2 (accurate reflection), Level 3 (mind reading — articulating what is unstated), Level 4 (validating in terms of history), Level 5 (validating in terms of the current situation), Level 6 (radical genuineness — treating the person as capable and whole). Validation reduces emotional arousal, strengthens the therapeutic relationship, and is a prerequisite for effective change strategies. Invalidation — even unintentional — escalates dysregulation.
short_summary: Describes Linehan's six validation levels from paying attention to radical genuineness as a de-escalating interpersonal technique.

title: Positive Psychology Interventions: Three Good Things
content: The Three Good Things (or gratitude journal) exercise (Seligman, 2005) involves writing down three positive events that occurred each day and the cause of each event, for one week. Meta-analyses confirm that the intervention reliably increases happiness and decreases depressive symptoms for up to six months, with larger effects when personalised and when clients identify their contribution to positive events. Mechanistically, the exercise shifts attentional bias from threat monitoring toward positive stimuli, increases savoring, and builds a more balanced self-narrative. It is easily integrated into CBT as a homework between sessions and is suitable as a standalone bibliotherapy exercise.
short_summary: Describes the Three Good Things gratitude exercise: three positive events plus their causes daily for one week, with lasting wellbeing effects.
contraindications: Active severe depression — positive interventions may feel invalidating; introduce after initial BA phase.

title: Expressive Writing: Pennebaker Protocol
content: Pennebaker's expressive writing protocol involves writing continuously about the deepest thoughts and feelings related to a significant emotional or stressful event for 15–20 minutes on 3–4 consecutive days. Participants are instructed to write about feelings rather than factual accounts. Meta-analyses show significant reductions in anxiety, depression, PTSD symptoms, physical health visits, and improvements in immune function. Mechanisms include emotional processing, cognitive integration of traumatic events (formation of a coherent narrative), and inhibition reduction. Contraindicated in acute trauma without adequate therapeutic support. Can be adapted for use as structured journalling homework in CBT.
short_summary: Describes Pennebaker's expressive writing protocol (15-20 min/day × 3-4 days) and its evidence base for emotional processing and stress reduction.
safety_notes: Brief emotional distress increase during writing is normal and transient.
contraindications: Acute trauma without therapeutic support; severe dissociation.

title: Social Skills Training
content: Social skills training (SST) is a structured CBT intervention for clients whose distress is maintained by skills deficits (not just anxiety or avoidance). Components include: instruction and modelling of targeted skills (initiating conversations, active listening, body language, turn-taking); behaviour rehearsal via role-play; performance feedback (direct or video-based); reinforcement; and generalisation practice (in-session role-play → real-world homework). Target skills are identified through behavioural assessment. SST is indicated for social anxiety, autism spectrum conditions, schizophrenia, borderline personality disorder, and social isolation. It is often combined with anxiety exposure to address both skills deficits and anxiety.
short_summary: Describes SST components (instruction, modelling, role-play, feedback) for social skills deficits across social anxiety, ASD, and personality disorders.
source_name: Bellack Social Skills Training for Schizophrenia

title: Postnatal Depression: CBT Formulation
content: Postnatal depression (PND) affects 10–15% of mothers and 5–10% of fathers. CBT formulation for PND incorporates biological (hormonal shifts, sleep deprivation), psychological (perfectionist parenting standards, identity shift, loss of valued roles, intrusive infant-harm thoughts), and social factors (isolation, relationship changes, lack of support). Common cognitive patterns include 'I should be happy, so there is something fundamentally wrong with me' and catastrophic misinterpretations of normal infant-related intrusive thoughts. Treatment includes normalising intrusive thoughts, behavioural activation, self-compassion work, and partner/systemic components. Infant bonding is addressed directly if present.
short_summary: Presents CBT formulation for postnatal depression covering biological, psychological, and social factors with normalising intrusive thought work.
source_name: NICE Guidelines for Perinatal Mental Health
safety_notes: Screen for suicidality and infanticide risk; liaise with perinatal team.
contraindications: Active psychosis (postpartum psychosis) — requires urgent psychiatric referral.

title: Medically Unexplained Symptoms: CBT Approach
content: Medically unexplained symptoms (MUS) / functional somatic symptoms are persistent physical symptoms without adequate organic explanation. The CBT model identifies perpetuating factors: attentional amplification (body scanning); catastrophic misinterpretation of symptoms ('This fatigue means I have a serious disease'); illness behaviour (rest, avoidance, reassurance-seeking) that leads to deconditioning and symptom amplification; and social reinforcement. CBT for MUS includes: biopsychosocial psychoeducation; graded activity increase; attention retraining; challenging illness beliefs; and reducing safety behaviours. CBT for chronic fatigue syndrome (GET + CBT) and irritable bowel syndrome have the strongest evidence bases within this category.
short_summary: Describes the CBT perpetuating cycle model for MUS and interventions: graded activity, attention retraining, and illness belief restructuring.
safety_notes: Full medical investigation must precede MUS formulation to rule out organic pathology.
contraindications: Undiagnosed progressive illness — medical work-up required first.

title: Resilience Building: Post-Traumatic Growth
content: Post-traumatic growth (PTG) refers to positive psychological change that emerges from the struggle with highly challenging life circumstances. Tedeschi and Calhoun identify five PTG domains: new possibilities (discovering new paths); relating to others (increased closeness and compassion); personal strength ('If I survived that, I can handle this'); spiritual change; and appreciation of life. PTG does not replace PTSD or exclude ongoing distress — it coexists with it. CBT facilitates PTG by: creating a coherent trauma narrative; identifying growth narratives within the account; exploring changed world assumptions; and fostering deliberate reflection on meaning-making. PTG is not imposed on clients but explored if it arises organically.
short_summary: Introduces post-traumatic growth (PTG) across five domains and how CBT facilitates growth through narrative, meaning-making, and assumption restructuring.
contraindications: Do not prematurely introduce PTG framing; ensure trauma processing is complete first.

title: Coping Strategies: Problem-Focused vs Emotion-Focused
content: Lazarus and Folkman's transactional stress-coping model distinguishes two coping strategies: Problem-focused coping directly addresses the stressor (information-gathering, action planning, problem-solving) and is most effective when the stressor is controllable. Emotion-focused coping manages the emotional response to the stressor (acceptance, reframing, seeking support, distraction) and is most effective when the stressor is uncontrollable. Flexible coping — using different strategies based on situational appraisal — predicts better outcomes than rigid adherence to one style. CBT teaches clients to assess stressor controllability, match coping style accordingly, and build a broader coping repertoire to reduce emotional avoidance coping.
short_summary: Explains Lazarus and Folkman's problem-focused vs emotion-focused coping distinction and the importance of flexible coping matching.
source_name: Lazarus & Folkman Stress Appraisal and Coping

title: Mindful Eating for Disordered Eating Patterns
content: Mindful eating (ME) involves bringing non-judgmental attention to the experience of eating: hunger and satiety cues, food texture, taste, and smell, environmental eating triggers, and emotional states preceding eating. ME-based interventions reduce binge eating, emotional eating, and disordered eating cognitions by: increasing interoceptive awareness of internal hunger/satiety signals; reducing automatic eating in response to external cues; reducing the shame and urgency associated with 'forbidden foods' through non-judgmental exposure; and breaking eating-as-emotion-regulation patterns. ME is typically delivered as a component of DBT-informed eating disorder treatment or as a standalone 8-session mindful eating group.
short_summary: Describes mindful eating interventions for binge eating and emotional eating via interoceptive awareness and non-judgmental food exposure.
contraindications: Active AN with medical instability — mindful eating is not a substitute for nutritional rehabilitation.

title: Meaning-Making in Existential CBT
content: Existential themes — death, freedom, isolation, and meaninglessness — can underlie chronic depression and anxiety that is not fully addressed by symptom-focused CBT. Meaning-making interventions help clients identify and reconnect with sources of meaning and purpose: relationships, creative work, service to others, spiritual or philosophical frameworks. Viktor Frankl's logotherapy concepts ('life has meaning in all circumstances including suffering') are integrated with CBT techniques. Interventions include: the 'meaning audit' (rating life domains by personal meaning); values clarification; and narrative work exploring the story told about one's life. Meaning-focused work is particularly indicated in adjustment disorder, grief, chronic illness, and existential depression.
short_summary: Integrates existential meaning-making (Frankl, logotherapy) with CBT for chronic depression, grief, and chronic illness via meaning audit and values work.
source_name: Frankl Man's Search for Meaning; Wong Meaning Therapy

title: Attention Bias Modification for Anxiety
content: Attentional bias toward threat is a core cognitive feature of anxiety disorders — anxious individuals preferentially detect and attend to threatening stimuli. Attention Bias Modification (ABM) uses computerised dot-probe tasks to train attention away from threat and toward neutral or positive stimuli across hundreds of trials. Meta-analyses show modest but consistent reductions in anxiety symptoms. ABM is most effective when: delivered over multiple sessions (8+); combined with CBT psychoeducation; and used as an adjunct to exposure. Clinical limitations include variability in training transfer to real-world situations. ABM is most promising for social anxiety and PTSD hypervigilance.
short_summary: Describes ABM (attention bias modification) using dot-probe training to redirect threat attention in anxiety, with evidence and clinical limitations.
